skill.name: api-patterns
skill.description: API design guidance for choosing REST, GraphQL, tRPC, authentication, response formats, versioning, rate limiting, documentation, and API security testing patterns.
skill.summary: Guide API design decisions across REST, GraphQL, tRPC, authentication, responses, versioning, and security testing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: AI review found the reported external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance alerts to be false positives from Markdown formatting, API terminology, and static regex examples. The bundled validator script reads local project files selected by the user, but it does not execute commands, access environment secrets, or make network requests. No evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in Markdown code fences, inline file references, and skill navigation tables. They are documentation text, not executable command calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every listed location was reviewed as Markdown content or inline code formatting. No shell invocation API or command execution syntax is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography detections match API design language, status-code checks, and documentation headings. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic implementation is present at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain prose, regex checks for HTTP status codes, or table text. The scanner appears to have matched short words inside unrelated API terminology.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Alerts Are Security Guidance False Positives
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts occur in REST status-code documentation, tRPC fit guidance, and an API security testing checklist. They describe defensive review topics and do not perform discovery or probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are static documentation text. No network request, port scan, process query, or host enumeration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Local Validator Reads Project Files
low_findings.description: The bundled API validator searches a user-provided project path and reads matching API files to report design issues. This is legitimate for validation, but users should run it only on intended repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script uses pathlib glob and read_text on files under the supplied path. It has no network calls, subprocess calls, environment access, or data exfiltration behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams often choose API styles, response formats, and versioning rules without a shared decision process. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for practical API design reviews.
actual_capabilities: Compares REST, GraphQL, tRPC, gRPC, WebSocket, and AsyncAPI trade-offs.
Provides REST resource naming, HTTP method, and status-code guidance.
Explains response envelope, error response, and pagination design options.
Guides authentication choices across JWT, sessions, OAuth, API keys, and passkeys.
Covers rate limiting, API documentation, versioning, and security testing checklists.
Includes a Python validator for local API files and OpenAPI specifications.
limitations: It provides design guidance, not a complete API implementation framework.
The validator performs heuristic checks and can miss project-specific issues.
It does not verify live API behavior or production configuration.
Security testing guidance still requires authorized testing and human review.

use_cases.title: Choose an API Style
use_cases.description: Compare REST, GraphQL, tRPC, and event-driven options for a new product API.

use_cases.title: Review API Consistency
use_cases.description: Check response formats, pagination, status codes, and documentation before release.

use_cases.title: Plan API Governance
use_cases.description: Define shared rules for authentication, versioning, rate limiting, and security testing.

prompt_templates.title: Pick an API Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between REST, GraphQL, and tRPC for this API. Ask about consumers, data shape, caching, team skills, and public access.

prompt_templates.title: Design REST Endpoints
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these planned REST endpoints for naming, methods, status codes, nesting depth, and response consistency. Suggest concrete improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Audit API Responses
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced consistency and error handling review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate my API response format, error structure, pagination model, rate limit headers, and documentation gaps. Return prioritized recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Create an API Governance Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an API governance plan covering style selection, versioning, authentication, authorization, rate limiting, OpenAPI documentation, and security testing.

output_examples.input: A public partner API needs broad client support, stable caching, and clear documentation.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use REST with OpenAPI. Define plural resource paths, explicit status codes, consistent errors, URI versioning, and rate limit headers.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript monorepo has one web client and fast-changing internal workflows.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: consider tRPC. It fits shared TypeScript types, rapid iteration, and internal clients, but it is weak for public multi-language APIs.

output_examples.input: A GraphQL API exposes nested user and billing data to several frontends.
output_examples.output: Review focus: add query depth limits, complexity scoring, field-level authorization, batching limits, pagination rules, and production introspection controls.

best_practices: Start every API design by identifying consumers, access patterns, data complexity, and compatibility needs.
Use one documented response and error format across endpoints unless a clear exception is justified.
Pair API design decisions with authentication, authorization, rate limiting, and documentation requirements.
anti_patterns: Do not default to REST, GraphQL, or tRPC without checking consumers and operational constraints.
Do not expose internal errors, stack traces, or inconsistent error shapes to API clients.
Do not skip versioning, rate limits, or documentation for APIs that external teams depend on.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete API code?
faq.answer: No. It guides API design decisions and reviews. Implementation still depends on your framework and codebase.

faq.question: Can it help choose between REST and GraphQL?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares consumers, data complexity, caching, public access, and team familiarity.

faq.question: Does it support tRPC guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when tRPC fits TypeScript monorepos and when it is a poor public API choice.

faq.question: What does the validator script check?
faq.answer: It searches local API files and OpenAPI specs for basic documentation, response, validation, and status-code signals.

faq.question: Is the security testing guidance offensive?
faq.answer: No. It is framed as defensive API review guidance and should be used only on authorized systems.

faq.question: Can teams use this for API standards?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help define shared rules for style, versioning, authentication, rate limits, and documentation.
